name: Postgrestool Workflow

nodes.name: PostgreSQL MCP Server
nodes.notes: This mcpTrigger node performs automated tasks as part of the workflow.

nodes.name: Sticky Note1

parameters.content:
## 2. Keep Secure by Preventing Raw SQL Statements
[Read more about the PostgreSQL Node]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

Whilst it may be easier to just let the Agent provide the full raw SQL statement,
it may expose you or your organisation to a real security risk where in the worst
case, data may be unknowingly leaked or deleted.

Forcing the agent to provide only the parameters of the query
means we can guard somewhat against this risk and also allows
use of query parameters as best practice against SQL injection attacks.

nodes.notes: This stickyNote node performs automated tasks as part of the workflow.

nodes.name: Sticky Note2

parameters.content:
## Try It Out!
### This n8n demonstrates how to build a simple PostgreSQL MCP server to manage your PostgreSQL database such as HR, Payroll, Sale, Inventory and More!

This MCP example is based off an official MCP reference implementation which can be found here -{{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

### How it works
* A MCP server trigger is used and connected to 5 tools: 2 postgreSQL and 3 custom workflow.
* The 2 postgreSQL tools are simple read-only queries and as such, the postgreSQL tool can be simply used.
* The 3 custom workflow tools are used for select, insert and update queries as these are operations which require a bit more discretion.
* Whilst it may be easier to allow the agent to use raw SQL queries, we may find it a little safer to just allow for the parameters instead. The custom workflow tool allows us to define this restricted schema for tool input which we'll use to construct the SQL statement ourselves.
* All 3 custom workflow tools trigger the same "Execute workflow" trigger in this very template which has a switch to route the operation to the correct handler.
* Finally, we use our standard PostgreSQL node to handle select, insert and update operations. The responses are then sent back to the the MCP client.

### How to use
* This PostgreSQL MCP server allows any compatible MCP client to manage a PostgreSQL database by supporting select, create and update operations. You will need to have a database available before you can use this server.
* Connect your MCP client by following the n8n guidelines here - {{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}
* Try the following queries in your MCP client:
  * "Please help me check if Alex has an entry in the users table. If not, please help me create a record for her."
  * "What was the top selling product in the last week?"
  * "How many high priority support tickets are still open this morning?"

### Requirements
* PostgreSQL for database. This can be an external database such as Supabase or one you can host internally.
* MCP Client or Agent for usage such as Claude Desktop - {{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

### Customising this workflow
* If the scope of schemas or tables is too open, try restrict it so the MCP serves a specific purpose for business operations. eg. Confine the querying and editing to HR only tables before providing access to people in that department.
* Remember to set the MCP server to require credentials before going to production and sharing this MCP server with others!

nodes.notes: This stickyNote node performs automated tasks as part of the workflow.

nodes.name: Sticky Note3

parameters.content:
### Always Authenticate Your Server!
Before going to production, it's always advised to enable authentication on your MCP server trigger.

nodes.notes: This stickyNote node performs automated tasks as part of the workflow.

parameters.content:
## 1. Set up an MCP Server Trigger
[Read more about the MCP Server Trigger]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

## 2. Keep Secure by Preventing Raw SQL Statements
[Read more about the PostgreSQL Node]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

Whilst it may be easier to just let the Agent provide the full raw SQL statement,
it may expose you or your organisation to a real security risk where in the worst
case, data may be unknowingly leaked or deleted.

Forcing the agent to provide only the parameters of the query
means we can guard somewhat against this risk and also allows
use of query parameters as best practice against SQL injection attacks.

## Try It Out!
### This n8n demonstrates how to build a simple PostgreSQL MCP server to manage your PostgreSQL database such as HR, Payroll, Sale, Inventory and More!

This MCP example is based off an official MCP reference implementation which can be found here -{{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

### How it works
* A MCP server trigger is used and connected to 5 tools: 2 postgreSQL and 3 custom workflow.
* The 2 postgreSQL tools are simple read-only queries and as such, the postgreSQL tool can be simply used.
* The 3 custom workflow tools are used for select, insert and update queries as these are operations which require a bit more discretion.
* Whilst it may be easier to allow the agent to use raw SQL queries, we may find it a little safer to just allow for the parameters instead. The custom workflow tool allows us to define this restricted schema for tool input which we'll use to construct the SQL statement ourselves.
* All 3 custom workflow tools trigger the same "Execute workflow" trigger in this very template which has a switch to route the operation to the correct handler.
* Finally, we use our standard PostgreSQL node to handle select, insert and update operations. The responses are then sent back to the the MCP client.

### How to use
* This PostgreSQL MCP server allows any compatible MCP client to manage a PostgreSQL database by supporting select, create and update operations. You will need to have a database available before you can use this server.
* Connect your MCP client by following the n8n guidelines here - {{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}
* Try the following queries in your MCP client:
  * "Please help me check if Alex has an entry in the users table. If not, please help me create a record for her."
  * "What was the top selling product in the last week?"
  * "How many high priority support tickets are still open this morning?"

### Requirements
* PostgreSQL for database. This can be an external database such as Supabase or one you can host internally.
* MCP Client or Agent for usage such as Claude Desktop - {{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

### Customising this workflow
* If the scope of schemas or tables is too open, try restrict it so the MCP serves a specific purpose for business operations. eg. Confine the querying and editing to HR only tables before providing access to people in that department.
* Remember to set the MCP server to require credentials before going to production and sharing this MCP server with others!

### Always Authenticate Your Server!
Before going to production, it's always advised to enable authentication on your MCP server trigger.

prerequisites: An active n8n instance installed locally, in a Docker container, or hosted on n8n Cloud.
Basic familiarity with importing JSON templates in n8n.
setupInstructions: Download the JSON configuration for this workflow.
Ensure you set up credentials for all connected integrations.
Test your trigger and run execution.
configurationSteps: Verify the settings of the trigger node "When Executed by Another Workflow".
Open the "GetTableSchema" node (n8n-nodes-base.postgresTool) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "ListTables" node (n8n-nodes-base.postgresTool) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "CreateTableRecords" node (n8n-nodes-base.noOp) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "ReadTableRecord" node (n8n-nodes-base.postgres) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Operation" node (n8n-nodes-base.switch) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "UpdateTableRecord" node (n8n-nodes-base.postgres) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "UpdateTableRecords" node (n8n-nodes-base.noOp) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "CreateTableRecord" node (n8n-nodes-base.postgres) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "ReadTableRows" node (n8n-nodes-base.noOp) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "PostgreSQL MCP Server" node (n8n-nodes-base.noOp) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note1" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note2" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note3" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Error Handler" node (n8n-nodes-base.stopAndError) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
No global variables node (Set node) is present. If you need dynamic constants, add a Set node after the trigger.
testingSteps: Click the "Test step" or "Listen for test event" button on your Trigger node.
Send a test trigger from your source service (e.g. fill a test form or trigger a webhook trigger).
Verify that the node canvas lights up green, indicating successful data flow.
Open the final destination node to confirm that output records were saved correctly.
instructions.expectedResult: Upon trigger activation, inputs will flow through the configured nodes and execute actions successfully.
troubleshooting: Check credential warning symbols. Most workflow issues stem from expired OAuth connections or missing API permissions.
Verify that the data keys coming from your Trigger node match the exact names mapped in subsequent nodes (e.g., {{ $json.body.email }}).
Open the Execution history tab in n8n to view logs and determine which node failed.
securityNotes: Never hardcode API keys, passwords, or authentication bearer tokens in the plain text fields of node parameters.
If you download or share this workflow JSON, sanitize any sensitive parameters or configuration nodes.
customizationIdeas: Add an email node at the end to notify you of successful executions.
Incorporate an AI LLM node to summarize trigger payloads before writing them to integrations.
